summary: This short blog post covers three topics: a teaser for Joel's article defending Not-Invented-Here Syndrome in great teams, an announcement of Fog Creek's new discussion board, and a reflection on UI design trade-offs when using underline-styled edit boxes instead of standard HTML input boxes. Joel acknowledges that replacing standard form controls with underlines reduces learnability but admits he finds them too visually appealing to revert. The post illustrates Joel's characteristic tension between advocating UI consistency principles and occasionally breaking them for aesthetic reasons.
key_points: Great programming teams often reject external code not out of arrogance but because maintaining quality and shipping on time requires owning your dependencies.
Standard HTML widgets provide consistency and learnability, which are core usability principles.
Aesthetic preferences can conflict with usability best practices, and even advocates of consistency sometimes choose style over convention.
Joel openly admits the hypocrisy of violating his own UI consistency guidelines because the underline design looks cool.
money_quotes: When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.
I like to make fun of designers who reinvent common widgets simply because they can.
The big advantage of using a standard HTML edit box is consistency — more people will recognize it and know how to use it.
It probably does reduce learnability, marginally, to use underlines for edit boxes instead of boxes.
But then again, they're really cool looking and I can't bear to switch them back to boxes.
key_insight: Even principled advocates of UI consistency will bend their own rules when aesthetics prove too compelling to resist, revealing the eternal tension between usability dogma and design taste.
